business_model: Multi-location fee-for-service and insurance-based dental practice with hygiene recall revenue and treatment plan acceptance driving production
known_problems: AR over 90 days has increased for the last two quarters
Insurance claim denials are reviewed inconsistently and often sit in shared inboxes
Recall reminders are still managed from spreadsheets and staff follow-up varies by location
sales_notes: Treatment plan follow-up happens manually and no one can say which unscheduled treatment is most likely to close.
operations_notes: Three locations share admin support but each front desk team uses a different process for reminders and billing questions.
collections_notes: Billing questions come through a shared inbox and there is no consistent cadence for overdue insurance or patient balances.
digital_presence: Website with appointment request form and Google Business profiles for each location
notes: Owner wants a practical first step and is worried staff already feel overloaded.

business_model: Consultation-led medspa earning revenue from treatment packages, repeat visits, and provider utilization
known_problems: Consultation-to-treatment conversion is not measured reliably
Packages are sold but usage and remaining sessions are tracked inconsistently
High no-show rate on premium injectable appointments
sales_notes: Owner wants visibility into provider pipeline value and which consultations are most likely to convert.
operations_notes: Front desk confirms some appointments by text, but follow-up rules differ by coordinator.
collections_notes: Deposits are collected for some services, but there is no clear view of expired credits or unused prepaid packages.
digital_presence: Strong Instagram presence, paid social campaigns, website consultation form
notes: Two senior injectors drive most revenue, so missed consults have outsized impact.

business_model: Recurring membership studio with upsell revenue from PT sessions and coaching add-ons
known_problems: Membership churn spikes after the first 90 days
Leads from trials and web forms are followed up inconsistently
Class schedule is full on evenings but underused in late mornings
sales_notes: Sales conversations depend heavily on the owner and there is no reliable lead stage tracking after a trial visit.
operations_notes: Coach schedules are built manually and quieter class blocks are not being repackaged or promoted.
collections_notes: Failed recurring payments are retried manually and some paused members stay unresolved for weeks.
digital_presence: Website with free trial form, active Instagram, local referral partnerships
notes: The studio is profitable but growth has stalled because retention and lead follow-up are uneven.

business_model: Field service company earning from dispatched jobs, larger install projects, and recurring maintenance plans
known_problems: Technician schedules are frequently double-booked or underutilized
Customers are not updated proactively when parts delays affect service dates
Invoices are sometimes sent weeks after work is completed
sales_notes: Maintenance membership renewals appear to be handled reactively rather than through a managed renewal process.
operations_notes: Dispatch is the main bottleneck during peak season and handoffs between office staff and technicians are noisy.
collections_notes: Billing lags after job completion, which likely delays cash collection and creates avoidable customer disputes.
digital_presence: Google Local Services, website booking request form, call-heavy inbound flow
notes: Owner says summer demand is strong, but operational breakdowns are hurting customer experience and cash timing.

business_model: Clinic generating revenue from provider consults, procedure bookings, and longer-term treatment programs
known_problems: There is a long gap between diagnosis and first treatment slot
Patient education content is outdated and providers explain plans differently
Payment plan follow-up happens ad hoc when cards fail or installments are missed
sales_notes: There is no clear CRM or pipeline view for patients who consult but delay treatment.
operations_notes: Treatment capacity feels constrained, but the team does not have a clear view of where the scheduling bottleneck starts.
collections_notes: Installment collections are handled manually and ownership is unclear when payments fail.
digital_presence: Website, WhatsApp inquiries, moderate Instagram activity
notes: Bilingual team; owner is open to automation but wants recommendations grounded in what the clinic actually has today.

business_model: Single-location pediatric practice with a mix of insured visits, higher-value sedation cases, and recurring recall hygiene
known_problems: Parents cancel at the last minute and open chair time is hard to refill
Treatment coordinators use inconsistent follow-up scripts after diagnosis
Outstanding balances linger after insurance EOB delays
sales_notes: Higher-value cases depend on coordinator follow-up, but there is no clear tracking of which proposed treatment is stalled.
operations_notes: Schedule gaps from cancellations affect provider utilization and the team has no clear waitlist process.
collections_notes: Insurance timing and parent balances create friction, and statements alone are not resolving older accounts.
digital_presence: Website, online forms, strong parent referral base
notes: High Medicaid mix means staff time is tight and recommendations need to be operationally realistic.

business_model: Membership-led medspa with additional revenue from treatment upsells and in-clinic retail
known_problems: Membership churn rises after the first three months
Retail attach rate at checkout is low
Staff scheduling leaves treatment rooms idle mid-week
sales_notes: Promotions generate interest, but follow-up from inquiry to booked consult is not tightly managed.
operations_notes: Owner believes room utilization could support more revenue, but no one is measuring the gap consistently.
collections_notes: Membership billing exists, but failed autopays and paused accounts are not actively worked.
digital_presence: Active social ads, Instagram-heavy brand, online gift card sales
notes: The owner wants growth without adding headcount immediately.

business_model: Clinic earning from booked provider appointments, rehab plans, and repeat visits funded by insurers, employers, and private pay
known_problems: Initial assessments are full, but follow-on plan adherence drops after week two
Front desk spends significant time chasing insurer authorizations and missing paperwork
Employer-funded invoices are paid slowly and status is hard to track
sales_notes: The clinic gets enough new assessments, but ongoing program retention is weak and cross-sell into longer rehab plans is inconsistent.
operations_notes: Admin workload around forms and approvals is slowing patient onboarding and clinician schedules.
collections_notes: Corporate and insurer receivables have weak visibility once invoices are sent.
notes: Clinic manager wants better process discipline before adding more marketing.

business_model: Boutique dental practice with high-value cosmetic treatment plans supported by hygiene retention and concierge-style service
known_problems: High-value treatment plans often stall after the financial presentation
Hygiene reappointment rate is below target
The AR list is small but includes repeat late payers
sales_notes: Case acceptance for larger cosmetic plans is a major growth lever, but follow-up after consultation appears inconsistent.
operations_notes: Front desk experience is high-touch, yet reappointment discipline is weaker than expected for a boutique practice.
collections_notes: The volume of overdue balances is limited, but repeat late payers create avoidable friction for a premium brand.
digital_presence: Polished website, before-and-after gallery, referral-driven cosmetic reputation
notes: Owner is more concerned with conversion and retention than with pure collections volume.

business_model: Class-pack and membership studio depending on recurring clients, intro offer conversion, and instructor utilization
known_problems: Intro pack buyers do not consistently convert into recurring members
Waitlists are active for prime classes, but daytime classes remain underfilled
Payment failures and expired cards are followed up manually
sales_notes: The studio has demand at the top of funnel, but there is no structured conversion process from intro offer to ongoing membership.
operations_notes: Capacity is uneven across the week and instructor planning is partly manual.
collections_notes: Recurring billing exists, but failed payments and package expiry follow-up depend on whoever notices first.
digital_presence: Strong Instagram community, referral partnerships, online class booking
notes: Founder wants to grow MRR without expanding the studio footprint this year.
